steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must complete a logical conclusion: the researchers hypothesized the density-size relationship would differ in medieval settlements, but found it held for all 173. The conclusion must follow from that finding — the hypothesis was wrong.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — although there tends to be a positive relationship between population size and development of core urban areas for both medieval settlements and modern cities, social institutions likely limited the rate at which population density increased in medieval settlements.: Claims institutions slowed density growth — but the text says the expected pattern held, meaning no notable constraint. ✗
  • Choice B — despite a change in the role of social structures over time, there is likely a notable consistency between medieval settlements and modern cities in underlying elements of the social and economic interactions among individuals that shape the development of core urban areas.: Directly follows from the finding: the density-size relationship held, so the underlying social/economic drivers are consistent across eras despite structural differences. ✓
  • Choice C — the social and economic dynamics that affect urban agglomeration were likely much different in medieval settlements than in modern cities, despite the similarity across time in population density patterns.: Contradicts the conclusion — if dynamics were different, the same pattern would need another explanation the text doesn't provide. ✗
  • Choice D — the constraining influence of hierarchical medieval institutions likely had a significant effect on individuals in some social groupings but little to no effect on members of other social groupings.: Introduces an uneven sub-group effect not supported anywhere in the text. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
